video_title: Why the Next Financial Crisis Could Change America Forever | U Got Options | Ep.12
gist: Host Jim Carson and ex-Dallas Fed advisor Danielle DiMartino Booth debate the path for new Fed chair Kevin Warsh amid structural inflation, an unsustainable national debt, and a $258T non-bank financial system. Carson argues the only endgame is monetizing the debt and ultimately backstopping/buying the US equity market via a Treasury sovereign wealth fund; Booth counters Warsh will resist QE and the zero bound, but agrees a deflationary liquidity shock would force money-printing.

themes.theme: Warsh's Fed: dissent, credibility, and the limits of rate cuts

insights.claim: New Fed chair Kevin Warsh inherits a divided FOMC with few allies, and outgoing chair Powell signaled deference, offering to step aside so Warsh can try to build consensus.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A fractured committee limits how aggressively Warsh can move policy; watch dissents in FOMC minutes as a tell on whether he can deliver cuts or hikes.
insights.quote: I think Worsh is walking into a hornets's nest of disscent. I don't think he's got very many friends on that committee.

insights.claim: Warsh will not return rates to the zero bound or launch QE because he resigned from the Fed board in 2007-08 in protest of QE, later calling it 'Robin Hood for the rich.'
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: If correct, markets pricing an eventual return to ZIRP/QE under Warsh are mispositioned; non-bank players betting on a return to zero rates may be disappointed.
insights.quote: QE was Robin Hood for the rich and that he wanted no part of it.

insights.claim: Booth expects Warsh to deliver a few 'plain vanilla' interest rate cuts in 2026 while explicitly avoiding the zero bound, potentially signaling 'two is the new zero.'
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A 'higher floor' framing for the policy rate would reprice the front end and steepen expectations versus a return-to-zero base case.
insights.quote: If he was to come out and say two is the new zero that would get a lot of respect on Wall Street.

insights.claim: Rate cuts are a supply-side tool that mainly benefits markets and the wealthy, not the bottom 50% who cannot borrow, so cutting will not relieve the households actually being squeezed.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Frames why monetary easing widens inequality and feeds populism rather than fixing it; argues the real lever must be fiscal.
insights.quote: Lowering interest rates for the bottom 50 bottom 50% of of of America does not borrow money. They cannot.

insights.claim: BlackRock argued over a weekend that the labor mandate justifies a rate cut as the Fed's first move, breaking with prevailing market consensus.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A high-profile dovish call from the largest asset manager is a sentiment signal that the cut camp is gaining institutional cover.

themes.theme: Structural inflation and a bifurcated economy

insights.claim: The US has a six-year-running, structurally driven inflation problem the Fed has been unable to control, rooted in populism, protectionism, and great-power conflict rather than transient factors.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: If inflation is secular not cyclical, conventional Fed tools fail and real assets/inflation hedges outperform over the cycle.
insights.quote: It's a problem that we've been facing that's been now consistent for six years.

insights.claim: Headline essentials inflation is hammering households while services and wages are disinflating, producing a sharply bifurcated economy where the top decile thrives and the bottom 50% cuts essentials.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: K-shaped data means aggregate prints mask distress; watch staples/discount retail and small-business margins for the real economy signal.
insights.quote: food food companies came out in the Dallas Fed survey then they were like we're seeing unit declines we're seeing traffic declines we are having our margins squeeze to kingdom come we cannot raise prices

insights.claim: For the first time, the average American worker is losing money on an inflation-adjusted basis (real wages turning negative), per a Financial Times article.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Negative real wage growth is a leading indicator of consumption stress and rising populist political risk into 2028.

insights.claim: Food CPI printed 0.0% in a recent month because households are literally buying less food, and small-business bankruptcies are surging across the Southwest.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: A flat food-CPI driven by volume declines, not price relief, signals demand destruction at the low end and a worsening small-business credit cycle.
insights.quote: CPI for food printed at 0.0% 2 months ago. Meaning people are buying less food.

insights.claim: WTI crude has fallen back to ~$90/barrel but remains roughly $25 above the ~$65 pre-Iran-conflict level, keeping an energy-cost burden on households.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Elevated crude despite a pullback sustains headline inflation pressure and constrains discretionary spending; watch geopolitical supply shocks.
insights.quote: We've come back down to $90 a barrel on West Texas Intermediate, but that's still way north, $25 a barrel north of 65 barrels before Iran.

insights.claim: Home prices have fallen for two consecutive months and rents are falling outside AI-boom cities, but prohibitively high mortgage rates keep the bottom 50% locked out of ownership.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Housing disinflation helps renters but the affordability lock-out persists; AI-hub cities are an emerging divergence in real-estate exposure.
insights.quote: Now, we have two months in a row with falling home prices. We have falling rents outside of AI affected cities.

themes.theme: The debt endgame: monetization and Fed-Treasury fusion

insights.claim: The US national debt is mathematically unsustainable at higher rates, and the only exit is inflationary monetization of the debt by the Fed and/or Treasury working in concert.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: A monetize-the-debt base case argues for owning hard assets, equities, and inflation hedges and shorting long-duration Treasuries over time.
insights.quote: the only way out of this debt mess and backstopping essentially the debt issues with inflation the way it is is ultimately to monetize the debt.

insights.claim: Carson predicts the Fed's true top priority will quietly shift from inflation/employment to financing the US debt, deliberately letting inflation run to monetize it.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Fiscal dominance over the dual mandate would un-anchor inflation expectations and erode the risk-free status of Treasuries; a regime change for portfolio construction.
insights.quote: they're going to start shifting to prioritizing the the the debt of the United States over even inflation.

insights.claim: Carson predicts a forced merger of the Treasury and the Fed, reversing the 1951 Accord that separated them, with Treasury Secretary Bessent (not Warsh) at the helm of the response within ~2.5 years.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: An effective end of Fed independence would mark a banana-republic regime shift; watch for Treasury actions that pre-empt or override Fed policy.
insights.quote: there there is going to be a force merger

insights.claim: Hank Paulson publicly warned on Bloomberg ~4-5 weeks ago that a Treasury crisis is coming and the Treasury market must be backstopped, which Carson reads as a deliberate official trial balloon.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: A former Treasury secretary flagging the need to backstop Treasuries is an under-discussed signal that intervention machinery is being prepared.
insights.quote: The most important thing by far that's happened the last four or five weeks is Hank Pollson coming out to Bloomberg and telling the world multiple times that we have a Treasury crisis coming

insights.claim: Booth argues Treasury Secretary Bessent could launch a massive buyback at the long end of the curve, lowering long rates and aiding the Fed without impinging on Fed independence.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Treasury buybacks at the long end are a tool to cap yields independent of the Fed; watch debt-management announcements and bill-vs-coupon issuance mix.
insights.quote: Besson could could easily launch a massive buyback... At the long end of the curve

insights.claim: Monetizing the debt by sending money directly to people bypasses the banking transmission mechanism, which is exactly why post-COVID direct deposits, unlike a decade of QE, produced double-digit inflation.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Distinguishes QE (asset inflation) from direct fiscal transfers (consumer inflation); future direct-transfer programs would be far more inflationary than QE.
insights.quote: the the the reason we got inflation taking off... was because... the government just handed it out directly deposited into individuals bank accounts

themes.theme: Financialization, leverage, and the non-bank time bomb

insights.claim: The global non-bank financial system reached $258 trillion in 2024, about 51% of global financial assets, and many private credit/private equity players are now too big to fail like banks were in 2007-08.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Systemic risk has migrated outside the regulated, Fed-backstopped perimeter; a private-credit liquidity event could force emergency money-printing.
insights.quote: as of 2024 the non-banking global financial system was 258 trillion. That's 51% of global assets.

insights.claim: The non-bank system does not want one or two rate cuts; it wants ZIRP reinstated and a QE relaunch so it can resume the leveraged 'financial games' that made it larger than the regulated system.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Identifies the real constituency pushing for zero rates; their pressure intensifies in any liquidity squeeze, a catalyst for policy capitulation.
insights.quote: The non-banking system wants somebody to... reinstate it so that they can keep on with all of the financial games

insights.claim: A 20% rise in roughly $300 trillion of global equity exposure creates ~$60 trillion of new collateral, dwarfing the Fed's balance sheet and the fiscal response, so the market itself is now the dominant policy lever.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Reframes equities as the system's collateral engine; policymakers cannot allow a deep drawdown, reinforcing the implicit Fed/Treasury put.
insights.quote: market goes up 20%. You create $60 trillion give or take of new collateral dwarfs the Fed's uh you know balance sheet.

insights.claim: On Feb 27 the 10-year Treasury yield closed at 3.96% amid a deflationary/liquidity shock; that night missiles flew, creating an overnight supply shock that pushed yields back above 4%.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Illustrates that policymakers treat a sub-4% 10-year (deflation signal) as a trigger; a deflationary liquidity event is what forces money-printing overnight.
insights.quote: The yield on the 10-year Treasury on February 27th that Friday closed at 3.96%... and lo and behold that night missiles flew.

insights.claim: A deflationary shock has accompanied every prior launch of QE, and policymakers cannot tolerate a deflationary cycle, so a deflation scare reliably precedes money-printing.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Watch for a deflation/liquidity scare (e.g., 10-year breaking below 4%) as the proximate trigger that unlocks the next round of easing.
insights.quote: Deflation shock has been... has accompanied every single launch of people.

insights.claim: Post-WWII, the average incoming Fed chair has seen an ~11% stock-market drawdown in the first three months of their tenure.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: A historical base rate suggesting elevated equity-drawdown risk early in Warsh's term, which could itself trigger a policy reversal.
insights.quote: on average an 11% draw down in the first three months... the average Fed chair has seen an average 11% decline in the stock market in the first three months of their tenure

insights.claim: Carson's central call: the Treasury/Fed will ultimately backstop and directly buy US equities via a sovereign wealth fund, the only 'elegant' solution that resolves the debt, populism, and bubble simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: If the state becomes a structural equity buyer, it caps downside in stocks and argues for owning equities despite bubble valuations; a Japan/BOJ-style precedent.
insights.quote: the response to this is to buy the equity market itself because there's no other way out. It's the only elegant solution for all these problems.

insights.claim: Carson sketches a scenario where a US sovereign wealth fund is seeded with ~$1T from Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac stakes, then levered and borrowed against to reach ~$5T within 18 months to buy US companies and fund infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Provides concrete mechanics and a timeline to watch; GSE-stake monetization and SWF capitalization announcements would confirm the thesis.
insights.quote: They've already have1 trillion or so dedicated from Fanny May and Freddy.

insights.claim: Carson frames state equity purchases as a form of UBI delivered as stock rather than cash, giving non-working people equity that supports markets, funds industrial capacity, and lets recipients sell shares to eat.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: A novel policy model that would channel transfers through asset prices, structurally inflating equities and changing how social spending hits markets.
insights.quote: instead of giving people ultimately just dollars you're giving them equity you're buying them in to the equity markets

insights.claim: Carson argues this model will drive hyperinflation and unprecedented asset inflation, with equities rising in nominal and even real terms, unlike the 1962-82 era when equities did poorly in real terms.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Contrarian to the standard 'stocks lose in real terms during high inflation' playbook; if the state buys equities, the usual stagflation rule may not hold.
insights.quote: this will drive hyperinflation... any form of printing money especially going into equity markets will drive a incredible amount of more asset inflation even than we've ever seen

themes.theme: Populism, China, and the dollar's exorbitant privilege

insights.claim: Protectionism (tariffs, immigration restriction) is the inseparable 'cousin' of populism, and it drives global conflict and commodity crises by forcing multiple nations to bid for centralized resources, mirroring the 1970s OPEC dynamic.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Links populist policy to a structural commodity/inflation regime; argues commodities and supply-chain bottlenecks are a durable, not transient, inflation driver.
insights.quote: protectionism is the cousin of populism. You can't have populism without some level of protection.

insights.claim: Populist fiscal pressure is set to go parabolic in 2028 as Gen X joins Gen Z and millennials at the polls, plausibly electing a figure like Gavin Newsom who would pay people not to work, tripling structural inflation toward double digits.
insights.speaker: Both (Jim Carson & Danielle DiMartino Booth)
insights.so_what: A 2028 political-fiscal inflection is a multi-year inflation catalyst; position for higher structural inflation and fiscal expansion into that election.
insights.quote: that spending could go parabolic in 2028... voters could vote in a Gavin Newsome

insights.claim: Record multigenerational household formation (highest since data began in 1900), driven by ~50% unemployment among the 2024-2026 graduating classes moving home, is fueling parental and youth political anger toward 2028.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Concrete social-stress data underpinning the 2028 populist-fiscal thesis; a leading indicator of demand for redistribution.
insights.quote: Never had this many multigenerational households in the history of mankind.

insights.claim: The dollar's exorbitant privilege is America's trump card versus China, since China does not currently want or hold the position for reserve-currency status, giving the US room to print and monetize.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: As long as no rival reserve currency exists, the US can monetize aggressively; watch China's de-dollarization moves as the constraint that ends this latitude.
insights.quote: China doesn't want reserve currency status at all... that is the trump card.

insights.claim: Monetizing the debt and merging Fed/Treasury would destroy the Treasury's risk-free status and the dollar's reserve-currency status, effectively ending the republic 'as we know it' even if it preserves the debt.
insights.speaker: Both (Jim Carson & Danielle DiMartino Booth)
insights.so_what: The cost of the monetization endgame is structural loss of safe-haven status; argues for diversification away from USD-denominated risk-free assets over time.
insights.quote: it destroys the risk-free status of the US Treasury and it destroys the reserve currency status of the US dollar.

themes.theme: The Fed put and the case for a cleansing crisis

insights.claim: Booth wrote a book arguing Greenspan created the 'Fed put,' distorting US markets with the expectation the Fed will always backstop them; she blames Greenspan, not the Fed's founding mandate, for the dysfunction.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: The Fed-put expectation is the moral-hazard engine behind financialization; its eventual failure is the tail risk that repricing depends on.
insights.quote: I wrote a whole book about Greenspan creating the Fed put and the distortions that that introduced into US markets

insights.claim: Carson argues the Fed's core function of smoothing the business cycle is self-defeating: suppressing crises removes the pressure Congress needs to pass structural reform, so a coming massive inflationary or deflationary crisis is the only way to cleanse and reinvigorate the system.
insights.speaker: Jim Carson
insights.so_what: Implies a major crisis is structurally necessary and likely; positions for tail-risk and for the eventual evisceration of over-leveraged 'bad players' in financialization.
insights.quote: the coming crisis... is ultimately going to drive a crisis which... is the only way to reinvigorate the system

insights.claim: Booth expects Warsh to keep rolling mortgage-backed securities off the Fed's balance sheet and to refuse to buy more MBS even in a cataclysmic event, sticking to Treasuries.
insights.speaker: Danielle DiMartino Booth
insights.so_what: Continued MBS runoff with no re-buying is a headwind for mortgage spreads and housing; a tell on whether Warsh holds the line on QE.
insights.quote: I think he's going to continue to roll mortgage back securities off of the Fed's balance sheet... I don't think he's buying more mortgage back securities.
